1: The Song of the Vineyard opens with the prophet speaking on behalf of his "beloved" (dôdî) — a term of intimate affection. Jesus retells this parable in Matthew 21:33-41, making explicit that the vineyard is Israel, the owner is God, and the rejected stone (Isaiah 21:42) is the Son. In John 15:1-5 Jesus transforms the vineyard into a vine: "I am the true vine, and my Father is the vinedresser" — where Israel failed to yield the grapes of justice (Isaiah 5:7), Christ is the vine that produces fruit in those who abide in him. The beloved/owner distinction in Isaiah anticipates the Trinitarian relation: the Father tends the vineyard through the Son.
2: "He dug it and cleared it of stones, and planted it with choice vines; he built a watchtower in the midst of it, and hewed out a wine vat in it." The preparation of the vineyard is extravagant — every provision made for fruitfulness. In Matthew 21:33, Jesus reproduces the tower and vat details from this verse exactly, making the connection unmistakable. The care invested in preparation is the theological ground of the owner's subsequent reckoning: the question in Isaiah 5:4, "what more was there to do for my vineyard that I have not done in it?" parallels the divine complaint underlying the incarnation — God gave everything, including his Son.
3: "And now, O inhabitants of Jerusalem and men of Judah, judge between me and my vineyard." The appeal to the hearers to judge their own case is a rhetorical trap similar to Nathan's parable (2 Samuel 12:1-7), where David condemns himself before he realizes it. In the NT, the parable of the tenants (Matthew 21:40-41) uses the same device: Jesus asks "what will the owner do to those tenants?" and the chief priests answer their own condemnation ("he will put those wretches to a miserable death"). The legal form — YHWH as plaintiff, Israel as defendant — is fulfilled in the passion narrative where the roles are inverted: the true Heir is put on trial by his own tenants.
4: "What more was there to do for my vineyard, that I have not done in it?" The rhetorical question has no answer — every provision was made. In the light of the incarnation, the NT reads this as the question behind the sending of the Son: "last of all he sent his son, saying, 'They will respect my son.'" (Matthew 21:37). When the son is killed, the question of 5:4 is answered eschatologically: nothing more was withheld, and the rejection of the Son reveals the full depth of covenant unfaithfulness. Hebrews 1:1-2 frames the Son as the definitive final word after all the previous provisions of prophets.
5: "And now I will tell you what I will do to my vineyard. I will remove its hedge, and it shall be devoured; I will break down its wall, and it shall be trampled down." The removal of divine protection is the judgment. In Luke 13:6-9, the fig-tree parable extends the vineyard's reprieve: "let it alone this year also" — but the final removal remains possible. The trampling of Jerusalem by the nations in Luke 21:24 ("Jerusalem will be trampled underfoot by the Gentiles") echoes this Isaianic trampling-down; the siege of AD 70 is the historical fulfillment of the removed hedge.
6: "I will make it a waste; it shall not be pruned or hoed, and briers and thorns shall grow up; I will also command the clouds that they rain no rain upon it." The cessation of rain anticipates the drought-judgment pattern of Elijah's ministry (1 Kings 17:1; James 5:17-18) and the two witnesses of Revelation 11:6 ("they have the power to shut the sky, that no rain may fall during the days of their prophesying"). The divine command over the clouds anticipates Christ who comes "on the clouds of heaven with power and great glory" (Matthew 24:30) — cloud-command is a prerogative of the divine Son.
7: "For the vineyard of YHWH of hosts is the house of Israel, and the men of Judah are his pleasant planting; and he looked for justice (mišpāṭ), but behold, bloodshed (mišpāḥ); for righteousness (ṣĕdāqâ), but behold, an outcry (ṣĕʿāqâ)." The wordplay seals the parable's meaning. Jesus's woe against the scribes and Pharisees (Matthew 23:23) — "you have neglected the weightier matters of the law: justice and mercy and faithfulness" — is a direct application of the vineyard's failure. The church as the new vineyard (Matthew 21:43: "the kingdom will be given to a people producing its fruits") must not repeat the bloodshed/outcry pattern.
8: "Woe to those who join house to house, who add field to field, until there is no more room, and you are made to dwell alone in the midst of the land." The first woe targets covetous accumulation. Jesus's parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16-21: "I will tear down my barns and build larger ones") applies the same critique. The "woe" form itself reappears in Jesus's Sermon on the Plain (Luke 6:24-26: "woe to you who are rich") and the woes against the scribes (Matthew 23:13-36), making the Isaianic woe-series the structural template for Christ's prophetic denunciations.
9: "YHWH of hosts has sworn in my hearing: Surely many houses shall be desolate, large and beautiful houses, without inhabitant." Jesus weeps over Jerusalem: "Behold, your house is left to you desolate" (Matthew 23:38; Luke 13:35) — the temple itself, the ultimate "great and beautiful house," falls under the Isaianic judgment. The divine oath (nišbaʿ Yhwh ṣĕḇāʾôt bĕʾoznāy) emphasizes the certainty of the word; Hebrews 6:13-17 makes YHWH's oath-taking the ground of Christian hope in the unchangeable promise.
10: "For ten acres of vineyard shall yield but one bath, and a homer of seed shall yield but an ephah." The agricultural curse reverses covenant blessing (Leviticus 26:3-5). In the NT, the seed-parable tradition (Mark 4:8: thirty, sixty, and a hundredfold yield) is the opposite pole — the kingdom of God yields a hundredfold increase where covenant-breaking yields a hundredfold reduction. Christ is the one who reverses the curse: his death is the seed that falls into the ground and produces much fruit (John 12:24).
11: "Woe to those who rise early in the morning, that they may run after strong drink, who tarry late into the evening as wine inflames them!" The second woe targets the culture of revelry that obliterates discernment. Jesus's warning in Luke 21:34 — "watch yourselves lest your hearts be weighed down with dissipation and drunkenness... and that day come upon you suddenly like a trap" — applies this woe to eschatological watchfulness. Paul echoes it in 1 Thessalonians 5:6-7: "let us not sleep, as others do, but let us keep awake and be sober."
12: "They have lyre and harp, tambourine and flute and wine at their feasts, but they do not regard the deeds of YHWH, or see the work of his hands." The inability to "see the works of YHWH" amid abundance is the diagnostic failure. Jesus's repeated call to "have eyes to see" (Matthew 13:16; Mark 8:18) addresses this blindness. The works of God that are unregarded are Jesus's own miracles — he reproaches the cities where he did his mighty works "because they did not repent" (Matthew 11:20). Luke 16:19-31 (the rich man who feasted daily and missed the beggar at his gate) embodies the failure of Isaiah 5:12.
13: "Therefore my people go into exile for lack of knowledge; their honored men go hungry, and their multitude is parched with thirst." The lack of knowledge is remedied by Christ who is the wisdom of God (1 Corinthians 1:30) and who taught "as one who had authority" (Matthew 7:29). The hunger and thirst of the exile are reversed: "blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be satisfied" (Matthew 5:6); "I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger" (John 6:35).
14: "Therefore Sheol has enlarged its throat and opened its mouth beyond measure, and the nobility of Jerusalem and her multitude go down." Sheol expanding to receive the condemned is reversed in the resurrection. Christ descends to Sheol (Acts 2:27, 31: Peter quotes Psalms 16:10) and conquers it from within. Revelation 1:18 presents the risen Christ holding "the keys of Death and Hades" — the one who entered the enlarged Sheol now controls its gates, and the gates of Hell shall not prevail against his church (Matthew 16:18).
15: "Man is humbled, and each one is brought low, and the eyes of the haughty are brought low." The repeated humbling theme of Isaiah is expressed in the Magnificat: "he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate" (Luke 1:52). The kenosis of Christ (Philippians 2:6-8: "he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death") is the ultimate example of chosen lowering — the one who could not be brought low from without lowered himself voluntarily, and was therefore exalted above all.
16: "But YHWH of hosts is exalted in justice, and the Holy God shows himself holy in righteousness." John 5:22-27 states that the Father has given all judgment to the Son "so that all may honor the Son, just as they honor the Father." The cross is simultaneously the judgment of sin and the exaltation of the one who bears it: "now is the judgment of this world... And I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself" (John 12:31-32). The "Holy God" who shows himself holy in judgment is the same Holy One whose holiness is mediated through the Servant.
17: "Then shall the lambs graze as in their pasture, and nomads shall eat among the ruins of the rich." John 10:1-18 applies the shepherd/sheep complex to Christ as the one who gives life to the sheep. Revelation 7:17 pictures the Lamb at the center of the throne shepherding those who came out of the great tribulation to "springs of living water" — the lamb who was slain becomes the shepherd who leads. The nomads eating among the ruins is the reversal of the land-grabbers of Isaiah 5:8.
18: "Woe to those who draw iniquity with cords of falsehood, who draw sin as with cart ropes." The third woe characterizes sin as something actively dragged along — habituated transgression. John 8:34 — "everyone who practices sin is a slave to sin" — captures the bondage that the cord/rope image suggests. The "cords of falsehood" anticipates the binding of Jesus at Gethsemane (John 18:12: "they bound him") — the one who frees from sin's ropes is himself bound by those who serve sin.
19: "Who say: 'Let him be quick, let him speed his work that we may see it.'" The mocking demand for divine action is the voice of practical atheism. 2 Peter 3:3-4 identifies this as the eschatological mockers': "scoffers will come in the last days... saying, 'Where is the promise of his coming?'" The mockers of Isaiah 5:19 reappear beneath the cross: "he trusts in God; let God deliver him now, if he desires him" (Matthew 27:43) — the identical demand for immediate divine vindication, refused so that the deeper divine purpose can be accomplished.
20: "Woe to those who call evil good and good evil, who put darkness for light and light for darkness." The fourth woe targets moral inversion. Jesus's warnings about false prophets (Matthew 7:15-23) and the Pharisees' attribution of his works to Beelzebul (Matthew 12:24) — calling good works demonic — is the NT manifestation. The warning about the unforgivable sin (Matthew 12:31-32) is the eschatological consequence of the Isaiah 5:20 inversion: calling the work of the Holy Spirit evil places one beyond the categories of repentance.
21: "Woe to those who are wise in their own eyes, and shrewd in their own sight!" The fifth woe inverts the wisdom tradition. Paul applies this directly: "Let no one deceive himself. If anyone among you thinks that he is wise in this age, let him become a fool that he may become wise. For the wisdom of this world is folly with God" (1 Corinthians 3:18-19). In Isaiah 1:19-21, Paul argues the gospel exposes human wisdom's bankruptcy: "I will destroy the wisdom of the wise" (citing Isaiah 29:14). Christ crucified is the wisdom of God that makes human cleverness foolish.
22: "Woe to those who are heroes at drinking wine, and valiant men in mixing strong drink." The sixth woe targets false heroism — courage redirected from covenant faithfulness to competitive drinking. The Gethsemane scene inverts this: the disciples sleep while Jesus takes the cup of suffering alone (Matthew 26:40). The cup of wine that the sixth-woe "heroes" master becomes the cup of the Lord's Supper (Luke 22:20) and the cup of suffering that Jesus drinks — the true heroism is receiving the cup from the Father.
23: "Who acquit the guilty for a bribe, and deprive the innocent of his right!" The judicial corruption condemned in the sixth woe is dramatized at the passion: Pilate, who "found no guilt" in Jesus (Luke 23:4), ultimately releases Barabbas (a guilty man) and condemns the innocent. The bribe in view is Judas's thirty pieces of silver (Matthew 26:15). At the cross, Isaiah 53:11 is fulfilled: the servant "shall make many righteous" — the one unjustly condemned acquits the guilty, reversing the corruption described here.
24: "Therefore, as the tongue of fire devours the stubble... for they have rejected the Torah of YHWH of hosts, and have despised the word of the Holy One of Israel." John 12:48 — "the word that I have spoken will judge him on the last day" — presents Christ as the Torah/Word whose rejection is self-condemnation. The fire that devours stubble becomes John the Baptist's announcement: "he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire... he will burn the chaff with unquenchable fire" (Matthew 3:11-12).
25: "Therefore the anger of YHWH was kindled against his people, and he stretched out his hand against them and struck them... For all this his anger has not turned away, and his hand is still stretched out." The "hand stretched out" refrain is a counterpoint to YHWH's hand stretched out in welcome (Isaiah 65:2; Romans 10:21). In the Passion, both stances are held simultaneously: the outstretched hand of welcome (Luke 15:20) and the hand of judgment are reconciled at the cross where God "did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all" (Romans 8:32).
26: "He will raise a signal for nations far away, and whistle for them from the ends of the earth; and behold, quickly, speedily they come!" The signal-raising to distant nations is the gathering of Gentiles — the great commission (Matthew 28:18-20). Revelation 14:6 presents "an angel flying directly overhead, with an eternal gospel to proclaim to those who dwell on earth, to every nation and tribe and language and people." The speed ("quickly, speedily") anticipates Christ's promised return: "Surely I am coming soon" (Revelation 22:20).
27: "None is weary, none stumbles, none slumbers or sleeps, not a waistband is loose, not a sandal strap broken." The invading army's readiness is applied in the NT to spiritual watchfulness: "Dress yourselves for action and keep your lamps burning" (Luke 12:35); "stand therefore, having fastened on the belt of truth... and, as shoes for your feet, having put on the readiness given by the gospel of peace" (Ephesians 6:14-15). The soldier who never stumbles anticipates Jude 24: "to him who is able to keep you from stumbling."
28: "Their arrows are sharp, all their bows bent, their horses' hooves seem like flint, and their wheels like the whirlwind." The battle imagery foreshadows Revelation's war imagery: the four horsemen (Revelation 6:1-8), the armies on white horses (Revelation 19:14). In NT eschatology, the sharp arrows of divine judgment are overcome by the shield of faith (Ephesians 6:16: "with which you can extinguish all the flaming darts of the evil one").
29: "Their roaring is like a lion, like young lions they roar; they growl and seize their prey; they carry it off, and none can rescue." The lion imagery applies Christologically in two directions. As judge, Christ is "the Lion of the tribe of Judah" (Revelation 5:5). As protector, John 10:28-29: "no one will snatch them out of my hand... no one is able to snatch them out of the Father's hand." The prey that "none can rescue" from the invader becomes the sheep that "none can snatch" from the divine hand.
30: "And they will growl over it on that day, like the growling of the sea. And if one looks to the land, behold, darkness and distress; and the light is darkened by its clouds." The darkness at the end of Isaiah 5 anticipates the darkness at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:45) and the Day of the Lord (Joel 2:2). Luke 21:25 uses the sea-roar and distress as signs of the Son of Man's coming — the clouds that darken in 5:30 are the same clouds on which the Son of Man returns (Matthew 24:30).
